subsystem: libgd GIF image decoder — the top-level GIF87a/89a container parser gdImageCreateFromGifCtx(), which reads a GIF stream from a gdIOCtx abstraction, validates the signature and logical-screen descriptor, loads the global/local color maps, and drives ReadImage()/DoExtension() to decode one frame into a gdImage.
provenance: Identified by the exact libgd public API name gdImageCreateFromGifCtx(gdIOCtxPtr fd) and the private helpers ReadOK/ReadColorMap/ReadImage/DoExtension/LM_to_uint/BitSet, the MAXCOLORMAPSIZE / LOCALCOLORMAP / INTERLACE macros, and the gdImagePtr/gdImageCreate/gdImageDestroy/gdImageColorTransparent types — all the signature of libgd's src/gd_gif_in.c. The header comment '/* sample: primevul_c_train_2584_fixed */' and the '/*1.4//...*/' markers match the historical Thomas Boutell GD 1.x GIF reader that libgd inherited.
trust_boundary: The gdIOCtx 'fd' is an untrusted byte source: it wraps a caller-supplied file, memory buffer, or network stream containing an attacker-controlled GIF. Every byte read through ReadOK() — the 6-byte signature, the 7-byte logical-screen descriptor (buf[4] color-table/size flags, buf[0..3] screen dimensions), the per-frame 9-byte image descriptor (buf[8] flags, buf[0..7] left/top/width/height), the extension type/sub-blocks, the color-map entries, and the LZW image data — is fully hostile and may be crafted. Nothing here is authenticated; the 3-byte 'GIF' magic and '87a'/'89a' version are the only gate before structured parsing begins.

what_the_code_does.explanation: Entry point of the GIF decoder. It takes a single parameter fd of type gdIOCtxPtr — libgd's I/O abstraction that supplies bytes from a file, an in-memory buffer, or any other source through function pointers (getC/getBuf). It returns a gdImagePtr (a heap-allocated decoded image) on success or NULL/0 on any parse failure. This is a public library entry point, so fd carries entirely attacker-controlled data.

The stack frame. BitPixel and bitPixel hold the derived global/local color-map entry counts. Transparent starts at -1 (meaning 'no transparent color'), later overwritten if a Graphic Control Extension declares one. buf[16] is the scratch buffer that every fixed-size header is read into (6, 7, and 9 bytes at various points — all within 16). ColorMap and localColorMap are 3-plane (R/G/B) tables of MAXCOLORMAPSIZE entries each; MAXCOLORMAPSIZE is 256 in libgd, so each is a 3x256 = 768-byte fixed array. imw/imh/screen_width/screen_height carry the logical-screen dimensions; left/top/width/height for a frame are declared later inside the loop. ZeroDataBlock is a flag threaded into DoExtension/ReadImage to track empty sub-blocks. im is the output image, initialized to 0 so the terminator path can detect 'no image decoded'.

Both color-map arrays are zero-initialized before use. The length 3 * MAXCOLORMAPSIZE (768) exactly matches the byte size of each 3-by-MAXCOLORMAPSIZE array, so this is a correctly-sized clear. Zeroing matters because a frame may reference the global map without a local one, and partially-populated maps must read as defined color 0 rather than stack garbage.

Reads the first 6 bytes of the stream into buf and bails out if fewer than 6 are available (ReadOK returns false on short read). It then checks the first 3 bytes equal the 'GIF' magic. This is the first validation gate; a stream that does not begin with 'GIF' is rejected. Reading 6 into buf[16] is safely bounded.

Bytes 3..5 of the same 6-byte header carry the version string. '87a' sets gif87a=1, '89a' sets gif87a=0, anything else is rejected. gif87a is later unused for security decisions in this function (extensions are handled regardless), so the version merely records which dialect was seen. Reading buf+3 for 3 bytes stays within the 6 bytes already read.

Reads the 7-byte Logical Screen Descriptor into buf. buf[4] is the packed fields byte: its low 3 bits (buf[4]&0x07) are the 'size of global color table' exponent, so BitPixel = 2<<(exp) = 2^(exp+1), ranging 2..256 as exp goes 0..7. This BitPixel is the number of global color-table entries. LM_to_uint(lo,hi) reconstructs a little-endian 16-bit value (lo | hi<<8, range 0..65535); it computes screen_width/imw and screen_height/imh. These signed ints hold the declared canvas size that later frames are checked against.

The top bit of buf[4] (tested via BitSet with the LOCALCOLORMAP mask, 0x80) indicates a global color table is present. If so, ReadColorMap is called to read BitPixel color triples from the stream into the ColorMap[3][MAXCOLORMAPSIZE] array; a non-zero return (short read) aborts. BitPixel here can be up to 256, and the destination has MAXCOLORMAPSIZE (256) entries per plane, so the count exactly matches the buffer capacity — the safety of this copy rests entirely on ReadColorMap honoring that bound.

The block-reader loop. Each iteration reads one introducer byte c. ';' (0x3B) is the GIF trailer and jumps to the terminated cleanup. '!' (0x21) is an extension introducer: a second byte selects the extension label, then DoExtension consumes the extension's sub-blocks and may update Transparent (the transparent color index) and ZeroDataBlock; the loop then continues. ',' (0x2C) is the Image Descriptor introducer and falls through to frame parsing below. Any other byte is skipped via continue. Note that a stream of non-',' non-';' bytes could spin this loop, and DoExtension's own sub-block consumption is where extension-length handling lives (not shown).

Reads the 9-byte Image Descriptor into buf. buf[8] is the frame's packed-fields byte: its high bit (LOCALCOLORMAP mask) says whether this frame carries its own local color table — useGlobalColormap is the negation. bitPixel = 1<<((buf[8]&0x07)+1) is the local-table entry count, 2..256, computed exactly like BitPixel. left/top/width/height are the frame's little-endian 16-bit position and size (each 0..65535), decoded with LM_to_uint. These signed ints drive both the confinement check and the gdImageCreate allocation.

This is the hardening/confinement check (the '_fixed' part of this sample): a frame whose right edge (left+width) exceeds screen_width, or whose bottom edge (top+height) exceeds screen_height, is rejected. It ensures a declared frame fits inside the logical-screen bounds decoded earlier. Because left, width, top, height are each at most 65535, the sums are at most ~131070 and do not overflow a 32-bit signed int, so the comparison is meaningful (all operands are non-negative). This guard is what prevents downstream code that composites the frame relative to the logical screen from writing outside its intended region.

Allocates the output image sized to the frame's width x height and aborts if allocation fails (NULL). width/height come straight from the untrusted descriptor (0..65535 each), so the allocation size is attacker-influenced up to ~65535*65535 pixels. im->interlace is set from the INTERLACE bit of buf[8], telling the LZW decoder to deinterlace row order.

Color-map selection and image decode. If the frame declares a local table, ReadColorMap loads bitPixel entries into localColorMap (aborting with gdImageDestroy on short read to avoid leaking im), then ReadImage decodes the LZW pixel data using that local table. Otherwise it requires that a global table was actually read (haveGlobalColormap) — rejecting a frame that references a non-existent global map — and decodes with ColorMap. ReadImage is where the LZW decompression writes width*height indexed pixels into im and maps them through the chosen table; that helper is the largest untrusted-data surface and is not shown here.

If a preceding Graphic Control Extension set Transparent (via DoExtension), the corresponding palette index is marked transparent on the image. Transparent is an attacker-controlled color index passed unchecked into gdImageColorTransparent. After one frame is decoded the code jumps to terminated — so this function decodes at most a single image frame per call (extensions may precede it, but the first image ends the loop).

Cleanup after the loop. If no frame was decoded (im still 0 — e.g. a stream that was only a terminator), it returns NULL. Otherwise it trims trailing 'open' (unused) palette entries by walking im->open[] downward from colorsTotal-1, decrementing colorsTotal for each open slot until a used one is found. If every color turned out unused (colorsTotal becomes 0) the image is destroyed and NULL returned. Finally the decoded image is returned. The trim loop indexes im->open[] within [0, colorsTotal-1], driven by the palette gdImageCreate allocated, so it is self-consistent with the image object.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Length-field-vs-fixed-buffer contract. BitPixel is derived from 3 attacker bits (buf[4]&0x07) as 2<<exp, reaching a maximum of 256, and is handed to ReadColorMap as the count of entries to write into ColorMap[3][MAXCOLORMAPSIZE]. Safety is exact, not comfortable: MAXCOLORMAPSIZE must be >= 256 and ReadColorMap must write indices 0..BitPixel-1 only. If MAXCOLORMAPSIZE were ever defined below 256, or if ReadColorMap uses a <= loop or otherwise writes BitPixel inclusive, the global color table read overflows the 768-byte stack array (out-of-bounds write, stack corruption). The invariant the implementation must uphold: ReadColorMap writes at most min(count, MAXCOLORMAPSIZE) entries per plane and count is capped at MAXCOLORMAPSIZE. This is latent because the helper is not shown.

The identical length-vs-buffer contract for the per-frame local color table. bitPixel = 1<<((buf[8]&0x07)+1) also maxes at 256 and feeds ReadColorMap writing into localColorMap[3][MAXCOLORMAPSIZE]. Same exact-fit dependency: an off-by-one in ReadColorMap or a shrunk MAXCOLORMAPSIZE turns a hostile frame's local-table size bits into a stack out-of-bounds write. Because both call sites share the helper, a defect there is reachable via either the global or local path. Confirm ReadColorMap's write bound once and it covers both.

Untrusted allocation size / resource exhaustion. width and height are 16-bit attacker values (each up to 65535) and are multiplied inside gdImageCreate to allocate the pixel buffer; a 65535x65535 frame requests ~4.3 GB. The confinement check at line 101 caps them only by the equally-attacker-controlled screen_width/screen_height, so it provides no real ceiling — a GIF can declare a 65535x65535 logical screen and a matching frame. Depending on gdImageCreate's own overflow handling, this is at minimum a memory-exhaustion / large-allocation DoS, and if any internal size computation (width*height*bytes) overflows before allocation it becomes a heap under-allocation feeding ReadImage's per-pixel writes. Invariant: gdImageCreate must reject or safely clamp oversized/overflowing dimensions and ReadImage must bound writes to the actual allocation.

Numeric-conversion / signed-arithmetic reliance (the CWE-681 angle for this sample). All of left/top/width/height/screen_width/screen_height are stored in signed int, populated from LM_to_uint's unsigned 16-bit result. The confinement test left+width > screen_width is only correct because those 16-bit inputs cannot make the signed sum negative or overflow — a property that holds here (max ~131070 < INT_MAX) but would silently break if LM_to_uint's width were ever widened or if these fields were later fed into 16-bit signed intermediates elsewhere. The invariant: every dimension must be treated as a non-negative value whose sums stay within the signed range before comparison, and the same non-negativity must hold at the gdImageCreate call. As written the shown code is safe; the risk is latent in any implementation that reuses these values with a narrower or signed conversion.

Untrusted extension parsing delegated to DoExtension, then trusted back. DoExtension consumes attacker-controlled sub-block lengths and a Graphic Control Extension can write *Transparent to any value it likes. That Transparent index is later passed unchecked to gdImageColorTransparent(im, Transparent) at line 129. If DoExtension does not bound its GetDataBlock sub-block reads, or if Transparent is set to an index outside the image's palette that gdImageColorTransparent then uses to index im->open/color arrays, the result is an out-of-bounds read/write. Invariant: DoExtension must bound every sub-block against the stream and Transparent must be validated against colorsTotal before use. Latent — DoExtension and gdImageColorTransparent bodies are not shown.

Unbounded block loop / denial-of-service. The for(;;) loop only exits on ';' (terminator), a successful image, or a short read. A stream consisting of arbitrary bytes that are neither ',' nor ';' nor '!' repeatedly hits 'continue' and reads one byte per pass; while each iteration does consume input (so it is not a pure infinite loop on a finite stream), a very long padded stream forces maximal work, and if the underlying gdIOCtx can return the same bytes indefinitely (e.g. a mis-implemented context) it never terminates. Invariant: ReadOK must reliably report EOF so the loop is bounded by the input length, and the caller must bound total input size.

summary: This is libgd's gdImageCreateFromGifCtx GIF container parser in its hardened ('_fixed') form: the header validation, the added frame-confinement check, the exact-fit color-table sizing (max 256 into MAXCOLORMAPSIZE), and the destroy-on-error paths are all sound in the shown code, so it contains no directly exploitable bug as written. The residual risk is entirely in contracts it delegates to unshown helpers — ReadColorMap's write bound, gdImageCreate's oversized/overflow-prone width*height allocation, DoExtension's sub-block bounds and the unchecked Transparent index — plus a latent signed/16-bit numeric-conversion dependency that stays safe only because the dimensions never exceed 16 bits.

reviewed_anchors.reason: attacker-derived global color-table entry count (max 256) used as ReadColorMap write count into a fixed 256-entry array
set by untrusted DoExtension and passed unchecked to gdImageColorTransparent
only ever filled by ReadOK with fixed counts 6, 7, 9 and single bytes — all <= 16, no overflow
single-byte introducer/label; no buffer contract
fixed color-table buffer whose safety depends on ReadColorMap honoring the BitPixel<=MAXCOLORMAPSIZE bound
same fixed-buffer contract driven by per-frame bitPixel
signed ints holding 16-bit attacker dimensions used in the confinement arithmetic (numeric-conversion contract)
gif87a is recorded but unused for security; useGlobalColormap is a bool derived from a flag bit, no buffer/length role
per-frame local color-table entry count (max 256) feeding ReadColorMap into localColorMap
flag threaded to DoExtension/ReadImage; no direct memory contract in this file
correctly gates use of ColorMap; frame referencing a missing global map is rejected (lines 120-122)
initialized to 0 so the terminator path safely detects 'no image'; error paths gdImageDestroy before return, no leak/UAF in shown code
length 3*MAXCOLORMAPSIZE exactly matches the array byte size; correctly bounded
6 bytes into buf[16]; signature gate; bounded
reads 3 bytes at offset 3 of the already-read 6 bytes; bounded version check
7 bytes into buf[16]; bounded
little-endian 16-bit decode stored in signed int; part of the numeric-conversion/confinement contract
single-bit flag tests on already-read bytes; no length/buffer role

reviewed_anchors.anchor: ReadColorMap(fd, BitPixel, ColorMap) (line 59) / ReadColorMap(fd, bitPixel, localColorMap) (line 113)
reviewed_anchors.reason: count-driven write into fixed color-table arrays; central length-vs-buffer contract
single-byte reads into a scalar; bounded
untrusted extension/sub-block parsing that writes Transparent; bounds live in the unshown helper
9 bytes into buf[16]; bounded image-descriptor read

reviewed_anchors.anchor: left + width > screen_width || top + height > screen_height (line 101)
reviewed_anchors.reason: confinement guard relying on signed 16-bit arithmetic not overflowing; correct here but latent CWE-681 dependency
attacker-controlled width*height allocation with no absolute cap; resource-exhaustion / potential size-overflow
boolean flag assigned to image; decode-order hint only
the LZW decode into im — largest untrusted-data surface; write bounding is in the unshown helper
unchecked attacker color index passed to palette API; folded into the Transparent finding
indexes im->open within [0, colorsTotal-1] allocated by gdImageCreate; self-consistent with the image object
correct: destroys and returns NULL when every palette slot was trimmed
loop bounded only by input/EOF reporting; padded/looping streams force maximal work (DoS)

findings.title: Color-table entry count (max 256) written into fixed MAXCOLORMAPSIZE array — exact-fit contract on ReadColorMap
findings.anchor: BitPixel = 2<<(buf[4]&0x07) / ReadColorMap(fd, BitPixel, ColorMap); bitPixel = 1<<((buf[8]&0x07)+1) / ReadColorMap(fd, bitPixel, localColorMap)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write (attacker-controlled count vs fixed-size buffer)
findings.invariant: MAXCOLORMAPSIZE must be >= 256 (the maximum of 2<<7 and 1<<8), and ReadColorMap must write at most min(count, MAXCOLORMAPSIZE) entries per R/G/B plane, indexing strictly within [0, MAXCOLORMAPSIZE-1].
findings.failure_mode: buf[4] low 3 bits (or buf[8] low 3 bits) = 7 yield a count of 256. If ReadColorMap uses an inclusive loop, or MAXCOLORMAPSIZE is ever defined below 256, the global/local color table read writes past the 768-byte ColorMap/localColorMap stack arrays — stack out-of-bounds write / memory corruption reachable from a crafted GIF header or image descriptor. Safety here is exact-fit, giving zero margin.
findings.what_to_confirm: Read ReadColorMap: confirm MAXCOLORMAPSIZE==256 (or larger) and that its per-entry loop is bounded by '< count' with count already <= MAXCOLORMAPSIZE, writing each of the 3 planes within bounds.

findings.title: Attacker-controlled frame dimensions drive gdImageCreate allocation with no absolute cap
findings.anchor: gdImageCreate(width, height) with width/height from LM_to_uint(buf[4..7])
findings.bug_class: Uncontrolled resource allocation / potential size-multiply overflow
findings.invariant: gdImageCreate must reject or safely clamp width*height (and width*height*bytes_per_pixel) so no integer overflow produces an under-sized allocation, and the caller/library must bound total pixel count to a sane maximum.
findings.failure_mode: A GIF can declare screen_width=screen_height=65535 and a matching 65535x65535 frame; the confinement check (line 101) is satisfied because it compares against the equally-attacker-controlled screen size, so gdImageCreate is asked for ~4.3 billion pixels — a large-allocation denial of service, and if any internal width*height*stride computation overflows a 32-bit size before allocation, a heap under-allocation that ReadImage then overflows during LZW decode.
findings.what_to_confirm: Inspect gdImageCreate for overflow-safe size math and a maximum-dimension guard; confirm ReadImage writes are bounded by the actual allocated pixel count, not the declared width/height.

findings.title: Untrusted extension sub-blocks and unchecked transparent color index via DoExtension
findings.bug_class: Missing input validation on untrusted sub-block lengths and palette index
findings.invariant: DoExtension must bound every GetDataBlock sub-block against available input (no unbounded/oversized reads), and Transparent must be validated as a valid palette index (0 <= Transparent < im->colorsTotal) before gdImageColorTransparent uses it.
findings.failure_mode: A crafted Graphic Control Extension sets Transparent to an arbitrary value; it is passed unchecked to gdImageColorTransparent, which typically indexes im->open[]/palette arrays — an out-of-range index is an out-of-bounds read/write. Separately, malformed extension sub-block length bytes can drive DoExtension's GetDataBlock into over-reads or unbounded loops. Both surfaces are reached from any GIF with an extension introducer ('!').
findings.what_to_confirm: Read DoExtension/GetDataBlock for sub-block length bounding and loop termination, and gdImageColorTransparent for a range check on the color index.

findings.title: LZW image decode into im is the primary untrusted-data write surface (latent)
findings.invariant: ReadImage must clamp every decoded pixel write to the image's actual width*height allocation, validate the LZW minimum-code-size and code-table indices, and bound the interlaced row-order arithmetic so no row/column index exceeds the allocated buffer.
findings.failure_mode: ReadImage consumes fully attacker-controlled LZW-compressed data; classic GIF decoder bugs (out-of-range palette index into a color map, decode writing more than width*height pixels, or interlace row computation running past the last row) become out-of-bounds writes into the heap image buffer. This function passes width/height and the chosen color map straight through, so the entire safety of the decode rests on ReadImage's internal bounds.
findings.what_to_confirm: Audit ReadImage's pixel-store loop and interlace pass for a hard bound against the allocated image dimensions and for palette-index range checks.

findings.title: Signed 16-bit dimension arithmetic in the frame-confinement check (numeric-conversion dependency)
findings.anchor: if (left + width > screen_width || top + height > screen_height)
findings.bug_class: Incorrect conversion / reliance on signed arithmetic staying non-negative
findings.invariant: Every dimension decoded by LM_to_uint must be treated as a non-negative value whose pairwise sums (left+width, top+height) remain within INT_MAX before comparison, and must stay non-negative wherever they are later reused (including the gdImageCreate call).
findings.failure_mode: As written the check is correct because left/top/width/height/screen_* are each <= 65535, so the signed sums (max ~131070) cannot overflow or go negative. The latent hazard is CWE-681: if any of these values were ever stored into a narrower or signed 16-bit intermediate (or LM_to_uint widened), the '>' comparison or the allocation could see a wrapped/negative value and let an out-of-bounds frame pass the guard. No exploit exists in the shown code; the finding pins the invariant the confinement fix relies on.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm LM_to_uint returns a 0..65535 value and that width/height/left/top are not truncated or reinterpreted as signed 16-bit anywhere between decode and use.

findings.title: Block-introducer loop bounded only by input/EOF reporting
findings.invariant: ReadOK must return false at true end-of-input so the loop is strictly bounded by the finite input length; the gdIOCtx must not be able to supply bytes indefinitely.
findings.failure_mode: A stream padded with bytes that are neither ',' (image) nor ';' (terminator) nor '!' (extension) drives one ReadOK-per-iteration with no other progress; on a normal finite stream this terminates at EOF, but a misbehaving or streaming gdIOCtx that never reports EOF (or a maliciously huge padded file) forces unbounded CPU. Impact is denial of service, not memory corruption.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify ReadOK/gdIOCtx getC returns EOF deterministically and that callers cap total input size.

audit_checklist: ReadColorMap: confirm MAXCOLORMAPSIZE >= 256 and the per-plane write loop is bounded '< count' with count <= MAXCOLORMAPSIZE (covers both global and local color-table paths).
gdImageCreate: verify overflow-safe width*height(*stride) math and an absolute maximum-dimension guard; ensure NULL is returned (as checked here) on failure.
ReadImage: audit the LZW pixel-store and interlace row-order arithmetic for a hard bound against the allocated width*height, plus palette-index range validation.
DoExtension / GetDataBlock: confirm sub-block length bytes are bounded against available input and loops terminate; ensure *Transparent cannot be set to an out-of-range index.
gdImageColorTransparent: confirm it range-checks the color index (0..colorsTotal-1) before indexing im->open/palette.
LM_to_uint: confirm it yields a 0..65535 unsigned value and that width/height/left/top are never truncated or reinterpreted as signed 16-bit between decode and use (CWE-681).
ReadOK / gdIOCtx: verify deterministic EOF reporting so the for(;;) block loop is bounded by finite input.
Error paths: confirm every failure after gdImageCreate calls gdImageDestroy(im) (as shown) so no image is leaked; verify Transparent is applied to a live im only.
